behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is when the tool misinterprets git's rename detection as separate delete+add operations. A pure mock of git diff output would hide this: you could hardcode 'this is a rename' and the test would pass even if the actual parsing logic is broken. Only by running the tool on a real git repository where an actual rename has been committed can you verify that git's rename detection is correctly preserved through the tool's processing pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real git repository, commit an initial file, then perform a git mv (rename) to a new name and commit it. Run the tool analyzing these commits. Verify that the output contains exactly one entry in a 'renamed' category with the old and new names, and that this file does not appear in the 'deleted' or 'added' categories.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is when the tool crashes or silently drops binary files because it doesn't handle git diff's special 'Binary files differ' output format. A mock could return sanitized diff output that sidesteps the binary-handling logic entirely. Only by committing actual binary files (images, PDFs, compiled objects) and running git diff on them can you verify that the tool's binary detection and error handling work correctly with real git output.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real git repository, commit a binary file (e.g., a PNG image), then modify it and commit the change. Run the tool on this repository. Verify that the tool completes without crashing and that the binary file is counted in the output (either in modified count or file inventory).

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: The real failure happens when the tool invokes git commands on a newly initialized repository and receives unexpected output or error codes. A mock of git log/diff could return empty data, but real git on an empty repository might behave differently (error messages, missing refs, malformed output). Only by creating an actual initialized but empty git repository and running the real tool against it can you verify graceful handling of this edge case.
behaviors.test_description: Create a new git repository with git init but without any commits. Run the tool on this repository. Verify that it exits without error and produces a valid empty report structure (e.g., zero counts for all categories or an empty change list).

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is subtle: the tool may correctly parse some git output but fail on edge cases like file modes, symlinks, partial hunks, or merge commits. A mock that returns hardcoded counts would always match hardcoded test assertions. Only by creating a real repository with a known set of commits (additions, deletions, modifications, renames) and comparing the tool's reported counts against git's authoritative log/status can you verify correctness across varied scenarios.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real git repository with a sequence of commits: commit file additions, then delete some files, modify others, and rename a few. Run the tool. Verify that reported counts for added, deleted, modified, and renamed files match the actual git history by comparing against git log and git diff outputs.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is when the tool uses different git commands (git diff vs git diff --cached) without normalizing results, or fails to handle both staged and unstaged changes correctly. A mock of git diff could return identical data for both cases, hiding the actual command-execution discrepancy. Only by creating a real repository where you both stage and unstage file changes, then running the tool in both states, can you verify that the output normalization works correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real git repository with committed baseline files. Create new file changes: modify some files and stage them, modify other files but leave them unstaged. Run the tool. Then unstage all changes and re-run the tool. Verify that the output (file counts and categories) is identical in both runs, demonstrating that the tool correctly reports all uncommitted changes regardless of staging state.
